author: Basil of Caesarea
translation: Blomfield Jackson (NPNF ser. 2 vol. 8), public domain
note: Basil enumerates the titles of the Holy Spirit — 'Spirit of God, Spirit of truth which proceeds from the Father' — as marks of His proper, divine dignity, part of the treatise's case that the Spirit shares the divine nature. DELICACY: the phrase 'which proceeds from the Father' states the Spirit's eternal procession (John 15:26) and must not be read as ontological subordination; in Basil the Spirit is co-equal and co-eternal, ranked WITH not under the Father and Son. Do not quote the procession clause in isolation. Verified verbatim against source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-13).
text: It is called Spirit of God, Spirit of truth which proceeds from the Father, right Spirit, a leading Spirit. Its proper and peculiar title is Holy Spirit; which is a name specially appropriate to everything that is incorporeal, purely immaterial, and indivisible.

author: Basil of Caesarea
translation: Blomfield Jackson (NPNF ser. 2 vol. 8), public domain
note: Basil's baptismal argument for the co-equal ranking of the Spirit: we believe in and are baptized into the one name of Father, Son, and Holy Ghost together — the lex-orandi ground of the Spirit's co-equality with the Father and the Son (cf. Matt. 28:19). A clean pro-Nicene statement placing the Spirit alongside, not below, Father and Son. Verified verbatim against source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-13).
text: For as we believe in the Father and the Son and the Holy Ghost, so are we also baptized in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Ghost; first comes the confession, introducing us to salvation, and baptism follows, setting the seal upon our assent.

author: Gregory of Nazianzus
work: Oration 31 (Fifth Theological Oration, On the Holy Spirit)
translation: Charles Gordon Browne & James Edward Swallow (NPNF ser. 2 vol. 7), public domain
note: Gregory's blunt affirmation of the full deity and consubstantiality (homoousios) of the Holy Spirit — the Cappadocian settlement that the Spirit is God, of one substance with the Father and the Son, against the Pneumatomachi ('Spirit-fighters'). A clean, non-subordinationist statement. Verified verbatim against source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-13).
text: What then? Is the Spirit God? Most certainly. Well then, is He Consubstantial? Yes, if He is God.

author: Gregory of Nazianzus
translation: Charles Gordon Browne & James Edward Swallow (NPNF ser. 2 vol. 7), public domain
note: The celebrated 'One... and the Three' passage — the classic Cappadocian statement of the Trinity as one undivided Godhead in three co-equal persons: to conceive of any One is to be carried to the Whole; the Three together are one undivided Light. A superb, doctrinally safe Trinity quotation (no subordination, no modalism). Verified verbatim against source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-13).
text: No sooner do I conceive of the One than I am illumined by the Splendour of the Three; no sooner do I distinguish Them than I am carried back to the One. When I think of any One of the Three I think of Him as the Whole, and my eyes are filled, and the greater part of what I am thinking of escapes me. I cannot grasp the greatness of That One so as to attribute a greater greatness to the Rest. When I contemplate the Three together, I see but one torch, and cannot divide or measure out the Undivided Light.

author: Gregory of Nazianzus
work: Epistle 101 (To Cledonius the Priest, against Apollinarius)
translation: Charles Gordon Browne & James Edward Swallow (NPNF ser. 2 vol. 7), public domain
note: The famous soteriological principle against Apollinarius (who denied Christ a human mind): 'that which He has not assumed He has not healed.' Christ took a complete human nature — body AND rational soul/mind — so that the whole of humanity might be saved. Affirms the FULL humanity of Christ united to His full deity. Verified verbatim against source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-13).
text: If anyone has put his trust in Him as a Man without a human mind, he is really bereft of mind, and quite unworthy of salvation. For that which He has not assumed He has not healed; but that which is united to His Godhead is also saved.

author: Gregory of Nyssa
translation: William Moore & Henry Austin Wilson (NPNF ser. 2 vol. 5), public domain
note: Gregory's signature argument that the single, undivided divine operation (origin in the Father, through the Son, perfected in the Spirit) reveals one Godhead, one power, one nature — so we do not speak of three Gods. DELICACY: 'origin from the Father, proceeds through the Son, perfected in the Holy Spirit' describes the taxis (order) of the ONE divine act, NOT a hierarchy of being; Gregory's whole point is co-equal unity of nature. Quote WITH the surrounding 'the Godhead is one' conclusion, never the taxis clause alone. Excerpt begins mid-sentence at 'every operation' (marked with a leading ellipsis). Verified verbatim against source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-13).
text: …every operation which extends from God to the Creation, and is named according to our variable conceptions of it, has its origin from the Father, and proceeds through the Son, and is perfected in the Holy Spirit.

author: Gregory of Nazianzus
work: Oration 29 (Third Theological Oration, On the Son)
translation: Charles Gordon Browne & James Edward Swallow (NPNF ser. 2 vol. 7), public domain
note: Gregory distinguishes the three persons by their eternal relations (unbegotten Father, begotten Son, proceeding Spirit) while insisting the begetting is 'without reference to time' — i.e. co-eternal, not a temporal or creaturely origin. DELICACY: 'Begetter'/'Begotten'/'Emission' name eternal relations of origin, NOT degrees of deity; read in isolation the language could be misheard as subordinationist, but Gregory's timeless-generation qualifier secures co-eternity and consubstantiality (contra Arius/Eunomius). Always quote WITH the 'without reference to time' clause. Verified verbatim against source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-13).
text: The Father is the Begetter and the Emitter; without passion of course, and without reference to time, and not in a corporeal manner. The Son is the Begotten, and the Holy Ghost the Emission.
